Reasons (core grounds 1, 3 and 4 in part)
78. The law on reasons has been dealt with on several occasions by the Supreme Court: Connelly
v. An Bord Pleanála [2018] IESC 31, [2021] 2 I.R. 752, [2018] 2 I.L.R.M. 45 (Clarke C.J.) (O’Donnell,
Dunne, O’Malley and Finlay Geoghegan JJ. concurring); Balz v. An Bord Pleanála [2019] IESC 90,
[2023] 3 I.R. 751, [2020] 1 I.L.R.M. 367 per O’Donnell J.; Naisiúnta Léictreach Contraitheoir Éireann
Coideachta Faoi Theorainn Ráthaoichta v. Labour Court & Ors. [2021] IESC 36, [2022] 3 I.R. 515,
[2021] 2 I.L.R.M. 1. It is settled law that main reasons on main issues are required, and that certain
issues are to be viewed objectively as main reasons given the nature of the process or decision at
issue – for example if it is in dispute as to whether key statutory criteria for the decision have been
satisfied.
79. As acknowledged by the commission in submissions, where the commission is disagreeing
with a council (Sherwin v. An Bord Pleanála [2023] IEHC 26, [2023] 1 JIC 2701 (Unreported, High
Court, 27 January 2023)) or with its own inspector on a point on which the grant or refusal of

22
permission turns, such an issue is virtually by definition a main issue, speaking objectively, such
that a decision not to give reasons on it would be held to be erroneous (Crekav Trading GP Ltd v.
An Bord Pleanála & Ors. [2020] IEHC 400 (Unreported, High Court, Barniville J., 31 July 2020);
Clonres CLG v. An Bord Pleanála & Ors. [2021] IEHC 303 (Unreported, High Court, 7 May 2021);
O'Donnell v. An Bord Pleanála [2023] IEHC 381 (Unreported, High Court, 1 November 2023);
Killegland Estates Ltd v. Meath County Council [2023] IESC 39 (Unreported, Supreme Court, Hogan
J., 21 December 2023); Ventaway v. An Coimisiún Pleanála [2025] IEHC 406 (Unreported, High
Court, 16 July 2025) and Phelan Walsh v. An Bord Pleanála [2025] IEHC 533 (Unreported, High
Court, Nolan J., 8 October 2025) at 53 citing Connelly v. An Bord Pleanála [2018] IESC 31, [2021]
2 I.R. 752, [2018] 2 I.L.R.M. 453 (Clarke C.J.)). In the case of the inspector, there is the additional
factor that by contrast with a normal case where the commission adopts the inspector’s reasons, in
a case where the commission disagrees it must come up with reasons of its own.
80. The commission puts the matter fairly well, if they don’t mind me saying so, in the following
submission:
“23. Further, it is acknowledged that where the Commission departs from the
recommendation of its inspector, that the standard is one of ‘meaningful engagement’
(Konisberry v. An Bord Pleanála [2024] IEHC 194 at §18-40, Morehart v An Bord Pleanála
[2025] IEHC 418 and Walsh v An Bord Pleanála [2025] IEHC 533 at §79-§82). What that
means in practice is that where the Commission does not accept the Inspector’s
recommendation, it must (i) come up with its own reasons and (ii) engage with the
Inspector’s rationale (Clonres v An Bord Pleanála [2021] IEHC 303 at §99) - that standard
was also met here. In other words, the Inspector’s recommendation is a main issue which
must be addressed (Ó Murthuile at §24).”
81. Reasons primarily arise under core grounds 1, 3 and 4 (in part).
82. Core ground 1 is:
“1. The Board’s Decision is invalid because the Board failed to indicate the main reasons
for not accepting the Board’s Inspector’s recommendation to grant permission contrary to
section 34(10)(b) of the Planning and Development Act 2000 and/or failed to provide any,
or any adequate reasons for the Decision contrary to the general administrative duty to give
reasons.”
83. The parties’ positions as recorded in the statement of case are summarised as follows:
“Applicant’s position
1. The Commission failed to provide reasons for departing from the recommendation
of its Inspector in deciding to refuse permission for the Proposed Development, given that
the (i) obligation on the Commission to provide intelligible and adequate reasons for its
Decision; (ii) duty is ‘enhanced’ where the Commission departs from the recommendation
of its Inspector; and (iii) any issue on which the Inspector would have granted permission
is a ‘main issue’ in the appeal which requires reasons to be provided.
2. In this case, the Commission’s Inspector produced three reports which contained
detailed and favourable findings on the site suitability, contextual height assessment, and
exceptional design quality of the Proposed Development. Despite this, the Commission
departed from the recommendation of its Inspector in refusing permission. In doing so, the
Commission only provided minimal conclusions, if any, regarding height, scale and massing
and did not provide reasons for rejecting its Inspector’s analysis. It is the Applicant’s position
that the conclusions and considerations provided by the Commission fall short of the level
of reasoning required by s.34(10)(b) of the 2000 Act and by the well-established
jurisprudence on the duty to give reasons.
3. Furthermore, it is the Applicant’s position that the Commission mischaracterised the
site as being located within an outer suburban area where there is a prevailing height of 2-
3 [storeys], contrary to the explicit findings otherwise of its Inspector. No reasons were
provided by the Commission for this departure from the recommendation of its Inspector.
The caselaw is clear, reasons must be sufficient to allow the Applicant to understand why
the decision was reached and to permit meaningful judicial review. The reasons provided
by the Commission do not meaningfully assist the Applicant in understanding the basis upon
which the Commission’s Decision was reached.
Respondent’s position
1. The Commission provided clear, comprehensible reasons for its Decision which
engaged with the submissions of the Applicant and the recommendations of its Inspector.
The enhanced duty to give reasons where the Commission does not accept its Inspector’s
recommendation is such that these recommendations become some of the main issues on
the application and therefore, in line with the established case law, the Commission is
required to address these recommendations in its Decision.

23
2. In the premises, the Commission did in fact adequately address these
recommendations. Clear reasons are provided as to why the Commission did not consider
the design quality of the proposed development to be of a high quality, nor that it would be
appropriate to diverge from the prevailing building heights of 2-3 storeys in the locality to
permit the development of an 8-storey hotel. The Commission noted that the departure
from the prevailing building heights was not in accordance with the provisions of the UDBHS
which had been incorporated into the Development Plan.
3. Read as a coherent whole, in a manner which makes sense, the Decision clearly
considered it appropriate to address the building height by reference to the locality, being
the eastern outer suburbs – as did the Inspector. In accordance with the Development Plan,
the Commission considered whether an increase in height would be appropriate, but
ultimately concluded that it would not, on the basis that the proposed development
constituted an unwarranted departure from the prevailing building heights in the locality,
contrary to the terms of the Development Plan and the UDBHS.
4. Contrary to the established case law, what Core Ground 1 seeks is an exhaustive
narrative of the reasons. In line with obligations, the Commission provided clear,
comprehensible reasons on the main issues such that the Applicant was aware of the overall
basis for the Decision, and why the Commission differed from the Inspector’s
recommendation. In the premises, no relief arises under Core Ground 1.”
84. Core ground 3 is:
“3. The Board’s decision is invalid as it failed to take relevant considerations into account
in its considerations relating to 8.7(8) of the Galway City Development Plan 2023-2029 and
the Galway City Urban Density & Building Heights Study 2021.”
85. The parties’ positions as recorded in the statement of case are summarised as follows:
“Applicant’s position
1. The Commission failed to have regard to a further relevant consideration, namely
§8.7(8) of the GCDP and UDBHS. Section 19.4 of the UDBHS relates to ‘East – Doughiska,
Roscam, Ardaun’. There are two issues which arise with the approach taken by the
Commission to the UDBHS in its Decision. First, the Commission is of the mistaken view
that the site of the Proposed Development is located within the Eastern Outer Suburbs. The
site of the Proposed Development is not located in the area marked on the UDBHS as Eastern
Outer Suburbs but is immediately adjacent to this area. Secondly, the Commission
repeatedly refers to a prevailing height of 2-3 storeys where no such prevailing height exists
(a) for non-residential development within the Eastern Outer Suburbs, or (b) at the site of
the Proposed Development which is not located in the Eastern Outer Suburbs. It is well
established that the Commission must correctly construe and apply relevant planning
documents and that misinterpretation constitutes an error of law.
2. In addition to the foregoing, the Commission failed to have regard to relevant
considerations, that begin the existing buildings in excess of 2–3 storeys (including Briarhill
Shopping Centre, the Clayton Hotel and Maldron Hotel), and failed to consider the UDBHS
guidance permitted increased height where sensitivities are low. As a result of having regard
to such irrelevant considerations, the Commission misdirected itself as to the law.
Accordingly, the Applicant submits that the decision is irrational and invalid.
Respondent’s position
1. The provisions of the Development Plan set out clearly that the building heights in
the UDBHS are a relevant consideration in the assessment of any application for
development. (i.e both residential and non-residential development). Therefore, in relying
on the building height guidelines in the UDBHS, as the Commission did, there was no legal
error. Further, in circumstances where the Commission clearly cited and applied §8.7 of the
Development Plan, the Applicant’s contention that this was not considered is plainly
unstateable.
2. The Commission correctly considered the application by reference to the criteria
applicable to the Eastern Outer Suburbs, consistent with the approach of the Inspector.
3. Contrary to the Applicant’s position, the Commission did not consider itself bound
by the building heights in the UDBHS but rather considered, with reference to the UDBHS as
required by the Development Plan, that exceeding the prevailing building heights (which
apply to both residential and non-residential development) in the manner proposed was not
in accordance with the proper planning and sustainable development of the area. As with
Core Ground 1, the assertion that the Commission mischaracterised the locality of the
proposed development is misconceived.
4. Further, any purported reliance by the Applicant on separate grants of planning
permission, or the provisions of inapplicable Local Area Plans, is, with respect, misconceived.
A separate grant of planning permission for a separate development cannot bind the

24
Commission, nor can the Commission determine the application by reference to a Local Area
Plan which does not apply to the area of the proposed development – although it is notable
that the proposed development was in breach of the LAP sought to be relied upon by the
Applicant.”
86. Core ground 4 is:
“4. The Board’s Decision is invalid because it misconstrued the requirements of the
Galway City Development Plan 2023-2029 in relation to the issue of car parking
requirements, failed to take relevant considerations into account and acted irrationally.”
87. The parties’ positions as recorded in the statement of case are summarised as follows:
“Applicant’s position
1. The Commission misdirected itself in relation to the provisions for car parking as
provided in the GCDP by failing to have regard to a relevant consideration, namely, the
National Planning Framework (‘NPF’). The GCDP mandates a performance-based approach
consistent with the NPF. The Applicant’s Travel Plan demonstrated that the site is adequately
served by sustainable transport modes, and the Inspector accepted this analysis. The
Commission rejected it without identifying any evidential basis or addressing NPO 13. The
Applicant contends that the Commission’s reasoning fails to engage with the mandatory
performance-based framework contained in the Development Plan and does not explain why
it rejected the Inspector’s finding in respect of same. The Applicant further submits that the
Commission improperly introduced new concerns not raised previously and without giving
the Applicant an opportunity to respond.
Respondent’s position
1. The Commission correctly interpreted the provisions of the Development Plan
providing for car parking provision, both in terms of the actual quantitative requirements,
and the terms of the provision allowing for divergence from these requirements.
2. Table 11.6 of the Development Plan requires hotels to provide 1 parking space per
room, 1 parking space per 8m2 of bar space, and 1 parking space per 10m2 of function
room space. In the particular circumstances of the proposed development, the Development
Plan thus prescribed a minimum of 260 spaces for the proposed development, which instead
provided for 193 spaces.
3. In the exercise of its expertise, the Commission exercised its judgment not to permit
a development which was in breach of the car parking requirements of the Development
Plan in circumstances where it considered that the public transport provision for the
proposed development was insufficient to warrant such a deviation from the parking
requirements. This conclusion was reached in circumstances where it was considered that
the travel plan submitted by the Applicant did not justify the proposed departure from the
car parking provision in the Development Plan.
4. Further, there is no obligation on the Commission to provide reasons for a departure
from the Development Plan, nor is the Commission bound in any application for development
by a previous application for development.”
88. While the complaint is generally framed as one of reasons rather than misinterpretation, it
may be helpful to set the scene in relation to the latter first. Sherwin v. An Bord Pleanála [2024]
IESC 13 (Unreported, Supreme Court, Woulfe J., 11 April 2024) (Charleton, O’Malley, Baker and
Murray JJ. concurring) emphasises a number of critical points:
(i) The importance of the development plan is well established: Attorney General
(McGarry) v. Sligo County Council [1991] 1 I.R. 99, [1989] I.L.R.M. 768 (Walsh J.)
(Hederman and McCarthy JJ. concurring); Byrne v. Fingal County Council [2001]
IEHC 141, [2001] 4 I.R. 565 (McKechnie J.) (Sherwin paras. 90-92).
(ii) “It is well established that the interpretation of a development plan is
ultimately a matter for the courts. Any misinterpretation of the development
plan by the relevant planning authority is an error of law which goes to jurisdiction.
It is also well established that the development plan is not to be treated as if it were
a piece of primary or secondary legislation emanating from skilled draughtsmen, and
inviting the exceptive canons of construction applicable to such material. Instead,
a development plan falls to be construed in its ordinary meaning as it would be
understood by members of the public without legal training, as well as by developers
and their agents, unless the document, read as a whole, necessarily indicates some
other meaning” (Sherwin para. 96, emphasis added).
(iii) As regards what is a contravention, “Certain aspects of the plan may have a high
level of specificity. In those cases, it may not be at all difficult to determine whether
what is proposed is in contravention of the plan, and it would only remain to exercise
a judgment as to the materiality of any such contravention. However, at the other
end of the spectrum, it is not uncommon to find objectives in a development plan

25
which may, to a greater or lesser extent, be properly described as aspirational. Such
objectives may be expressed in general terms. In such cases, a much greater degree
of judgment may need to be exercised as to whether the development proposed
amounts to a material contravention of the development plan.” (Sherwin para. 95
citing Maye v. Sligo Borough Council [2007] IEHC 146, [2007] 4 I.R. 678 (Clarke
J.)).
(iv) “the first question must be the nature of the determination (if any) actually made
by the decision-maker, ‘as to whether or not the proposed application as a
matter of law and fact would materially contravene the development plan’,
(per Costello J. in [South-West Regional Shopping Centre Promotion Association
Limited v. An Bord Pleanála [2016] IEHC 84]), in circumstances where there has
been the required focus by the decision-maker on the specific provision of
the plan allegedly materially contravened. In my opinion that question is the
crucial starting point, before one gets to the questions as to the standard of review
by the court.” (Sherwin para. 105, emphasis added). See also Wilson v. An Bord
Pleanála [2025] IEHC 523 (Unreported, High Court, 3 October 2025) per Farrell J.
(v) Thus where “the details of this planning application, and the materials before the
Inspector and the Board, clearly required a focus by the Board on [a specified]
policy” (Sherwin para. 106), this has the consequence that, even if the material was
“not expressly alleging a material contravention of the development plan in terms”
(Sherwin para. 107), the position is that “[i]n the circumstances it [is] incumbent
on the Inspector to make a determination as to whether or not the proposed
development, as a matter of law and fact, would materially contravene [the
specified] policy” (Sherwin para. 108, emphasis added).
(vi) “the first step in making the required determination was for the Inspector to
consider the text of [the] policy … and to interpret that text to some degree, in
terms of what she understood it to mean. In many cases the interpretation of such
a provision may be clear and even self-evident, but in other cases there may be
some lack of clarity requiring some level of engagement with the text.” (Sherwin
para. 109, emphasis added). “it was necessary for the Inspector to give some
indication as to her understanding of what the test meant, before she
moved on to her application of that test, as so interpreted, to the facts of this
planning application.” (Sherwin para. 110, emphasis added).
(vii) These requirements are not discharged merely by a planning assessment
that permission should – or indeed should not - be granted. Thus in Sherwin,
“opinion as to Block D1 comfortably sitting side by side with existing protected
structures, without detriment to their character, cannot be viewed as an implicit or
indirect assessment of compliance with policy CHC2. It appears from the sentences
which followed this opinion that the opinion is essentially based on one
consideration, that structures from different periods can co-exist within the same
land parcel etc, rather than based on the considerations expressly stipulated in policy
CHC2.” (para. 114).
89. We can then look at the reasons complaint under a number of headings.
Lack of reasons in relation to whether the project was within the Doughiska outer
suburban area
90. The commission argues that issues regarding the site location criteria of the plan were not
pleaded as errors of interpretation but rather as ones of reasons:
“31. The Commission agreed with the Inspector as regards the need to assess the site
by reference to the criteria relevant to the Outer Suburbs. Notably, this conclusion is not
under challenge in these proceedings. In other words, it is common case between everyone
that the correct criteria by which the application had to be assessed is that relating to the
Outer Suburbs. The only issue which arises in the proceedings relates to the reasons for the
Commission's assessment, in respect of which they disagreed with the Inspector.”
91. The applicant however is not accepting that the outer suburb criteria apply and is impliedly
putting that in issue to the limited extent of complaining about the commission disagreeing with the
inspector. Sub-grounds 18 to 20 in the applicant’s statement of grounds provide (emphasis added):
“18. The Board’s decision is expressly premised on the assertion that the proposed
development is contrary to Policy 8.7(8) of the City Development Plan which seeks to adhere
to the Galway City Urban Density and Building Height Study (2021) (the ‘UDBHS’). The
Board’s finding in this regard was based on the Spatial Strategy for the Outer Suburbs set
out in the UDBHS, which states that the prevailing 2-3 storey building height pattern should
continue and any opportunities to increase the height of new buildings above the prevailing
height in the area should be marginal.

26
19. The Board did not address the fact that Inspector expressly found that the
subject site was not comprised in the Outer Suburbs (but was rather closely aligned
thereto) and that, in any event, the UDBHS was expressly stated to be a guide as
opposed to an absolute measure. At §2.3.5 of the Addendum Report, this is noted in
terms by the Inspector:
‘Section 8.8 of the CDP states that suggested ranges of scale and intensity outlined within
the Urban Density and Building Heights Study are a guide and are not absolute measures to
be pursued or achieved and each site should be considered on its merits. The proposed 8-
storey over basement height clearly exceeds the envisaged prevailing height for the lands
to the south, but I consider there are other factors to be considered.’
20. Indeed, §8.8 of the GCDP demonstrates very clearly that where assessed in detail
against other relevant policy and guidance, as was the case here across three Inspector’s
Reports, lower or higher densities and heights than those outlined are available on a case-
by-case basis:
‘These suggested ranges of scale and intensity are a guide and are not absolute measures
to be pursued or achieved and each site should be considered on its merits. Densities and
heights lower or higher than those outlined in the study may be appropriate when assessed
against other relevant policy and guidance.’”
92. The inspector very carefully noted at 3.2.6 that this site fell outside the defined outer
suburban area, and phrased the matter, accurately, by saying that it was “most closely aligned” with
that area. The commission contradicted this subtle but important distinction by stating baldly that
this was an outer suburban area. One can’t say that that distinction makes no difference, if for no
other reason than that the relevant policies for the Doughiska area do not formally apply.
93. Clearly the fact that the project fell outside the outer suburban area was a factor for the
inspector in creating flexibility that would allow approval. The commission did not give reasons as
to why location outside that area (and thus the non-application of the development criteria for that
area) did not add to the flexibilities available.
94. The commission incorrectly argues:
“43. The Applicant places significant emphasis on the fact that the Inspector noted that
the location of the proposed development was ‘most closely aligned to the Outer Suburbs
designation’ while the Commission Order records that the proposed development was
located within the Outer Suburbs. Leaving aside the fact that the Commission is correct
about the location of the proposed development, the distinction between the Inspector and
the Commission is one without a difference. Both approached the issue on the basis that
the assessment had to be carried out by reference to the criteria relevant to the Outer
Suburbs. Consequently, no obligation to give reasons for doing something differently than
the Inspector arose. Notably, the Applicant does not allege any error in the interpretation
of the Development Plan as regards the assessment of the application by reference to the
criteria for the Outer Suburbs. The challenge is limited to a reasons challenge.”
95. The distinction is not one without a difference because it is not correct that the assessment
“had to be” (i.e., was required by an imperative instrument to be) carried out by reference to an
imperative instrument that did not apply. Even if the instrument is taken as a guide, by analogy,
the very fact that it does not apply gives a degree of flexibility that would not exist if it did apply.
The applicant phrases the matter as one of reasons (in effect, that comes down to a lack of reasons
for not regarding the formal non-application of the outer suburban policy as creating more flexibility).
It would have been more logical and intellectually satisfying had this been phrased as an error of
interpretation (in concluding that the development site was within the outer suburban area), but it
comes to the same thing either way.
Lack of reasons in relation to lack of sensitivity in receiving environment
96. The commission argues:
“38. §46(a) refers to 3.2.11 of the Inspector’s Second Addendum Report dated 5
November 2024 and suggests that the Commission failed to address that ‘there are no
sensitive aspects to the receiving environment that require protection from the development
of tall buildings’. At §3.2.11 of the Second Addendum Report dated 5 November 2024 the
Inspector addresses the visual impact of the proposed development as regards protected
views and notes that ‘the development will be a significant intervention in a number of the
available views. I also agree with the submission that there is no sensitive aspect of the
receiving environment that requires protection from the development of a tall building’. No
issue arises in this respect as the absence of a sensitive aspect of the receiving environment
that requires protection is not relevant to the central conclusion of the Commission, namely
that the proposed development was not consistent with the prevailing height of the
surrounding area and that the design of the proposed development was not to an exceptional
standard to justify such an increase in height.”

27
97. The problem with that argument is that it is a misunderstanding to say that the sensitivity
of the receiving environment was “not relevant” to the height issue. The receiving environment is
always relevant, particularly when there are flexibilities in the plan (due to this being formally outside
the policies for outer suburbs, and due to section 8.8).
98. The commission makes no reference to the crucial issue that this is not a sensitive location,
specifically not located within a sensitive or protected landscape or conservation area as identified
by the inspector, and gives no reasons why this is not an important factor in favour of the applicant.
99. The commission did not address the flexibilities in the CDP in the context of issues such as
those raised by the applicant and discussed by the inspector. That flexibility can be viewed through
the lens of the commission’s failure to address the inspector’s reliance on section 8.8 of the CDP
which allows departure from the policies at 8.7(8).
Lack of reasons in relation to not applying section 8.8 of the CDP
100. Insofar as the commission went on to launch the assumption that exceeding the “prevailing
height in this outer suburban area” is “therefore … contrary to the Galway City Urban Density and
Building Height Study (2021), [and] the Galway City Development Plan 2023 – 2029” (emphasis
added), if words mean anything that is an assertion that a finding of non-compliance with the text
concerned gives rise to a contravention, which implies an unreasoned unwillingness to
acknowledge, still less apply, the flexibilities in section 8.8.
101. Indeed much of the commission’s decision incorrectly uses the language of obligation. It
held that “the proposed development would be contrary to Policy 8.7(8) which requires adherence
to Galway City Urban Density and Building Height Study 2021” (emphasis added). The CDP does
not “require adherence” because section 8.8 goes on to allow for flexibility.
102. The discussion omits any consideration of section 8.8 which was central to the inspector’s
reasoning and which has the effect that non-compliance may be permissible on a case-by-case
examination notwithstanding exceeding the prevailing height. Such excess does not in itself
(“therefore”) amount to non-compliance with the CDP.
103. In relation to the complaint of not giving reasons for not applying this flexibility and thus in
effect of considering itself bound by the development criteria for the outer suburbs, the commission
submits:
“68. Further, insofar as the Applicant alleges that the Commission wrongly considered
itself bound by the heights set out in the Building Height Study, it is clear from the Decision
that this contention is incorrect and wholly misconceived. Rather than considering itself
bound by these building heights, the Commission clearly considered whether it would be
preferable to exceed the heights set out in the Building Height Study and concluded that it
would not, by reference to the mass, scale and form of the proposed developments, its
relationship with the surrounding area, and the absence of any justification in national policy
permitting such a divergence from the building heights, noting in particular that the proposed
development was in an outer suburban area on the periphery of the built-up area.”
104. That defence unfortunately highlights what is missing from the decision. It is only national
and regional policy that was considered to be not outweighing – the flexibility in city policy was not
even referred to despite being clearly a major issue for the inspector. The applicant makes this
point expressly in sub-ground 23:
“23. Finally, the Board does not outline, beyond reference to the National Planning
Framework and National and Western Regional Spatial and Economic Strategy by name, how
the policies contained therein did or did not justify a departure from the prevailing two-three
storey character of the of the surrounding area (which in any event is not the correct
standard to be applied, as is discussed in further detail below.)
24. The Board is obliged to state the main reasons for its decision where it disagrees
with the findings of its Inspector in this regard, notwithstanding the fact that the Inspector
had found the proposed building height of the development to be acceptable in three distinct,
thoroughly reasoned reports.
25. Furthermore, no information has been provided by the Board such that the Applicant
can understand why the Board differed from its Inspector or assess whether there was any
basis for suggesting that the Board's decision is unsustainable.”
105. Insofar as it was argued at the hearing that the statement “the policies in the National
Planning Framework or the National and Western Regional Spatial and Economic Strategy regarding
the promotion of compact settlement growth did not justify such a fundamental departure from the
prevailing two-three storey character of the surrounding area” is a finding that this is not compact
growth and thus a finding that consideration of the 2018 guidelines fails at the first hurdle, that is
complete post hoc reprogramming of the decision. The most natural interpretation is an
acknowledgement that policies favour the project but that these were outweighed by the character
of the surrounding area. But no reasons are given for not going on to consider the performance
criteria in such policies before coming to a conclusion of insufficiency. Admittedly the applicant

28
didn’t plead SPPR 3 but even leaving that aside, the policy does require a criteria-based assessment,
which the inspector engaged in but the commission did not.
106. The commission, in its wholesome, ongoing and welcome attempts to assist the court in
keeping the jurisprudence consistent, forcefully urges the court not to view a failure to make
exceptions as being something that requires reasons:
“77. Insofar as the Applicant complains that the Commission did not give reasons for
allowing a departure from the Development Plan standards, no such obligation is placed on
the Commission in that regard. The Commission relies on AAI Baneshane at §119 ...”
107. Here we come to a delicate subtlety. AAI Baneshane (I) was dealing with a different
situation, which was that the commission had come to a lawful view that the application as made
should be rejected. The point on which they disagreed with the inspector (that was addressed at
para. 119) was whether the project could be saved by conditions. The point there was that there
was no obligation to give reasons for not operating such an exception. But we need to be careful
not to overgeneralise from any given snippet of jurisprudence – it depends what the “exception” is.
108. The commission’s power to save a proposal by conditioning it is essentially discretionary.
Empirically perhaps it could be within the realm of the reasonable to suggest that they may be a bit
sparing about it at the moment, if caselaw is anything to go by. But be that as it may, it remains a
matter of discretion, and a lawful decision on a project doesn’t become unlawful because they don’t
give reasons for not reconfiguring the project, which they are not obliged to do. The fact that an
inspector wanted such reconfiguration doesn’t create an obligation to give reasons which would not
otherwise exist – given the nature of the discretionary option we are talking about.
109. In addition, many policies across government have clauses for exceptional cases. It would
be unworkable if after analysing the policy, a decision-taker had to give reasons for not regarding
the case at hand as being exceptional. Reasons as such are not generally called for in that type of
context.
110. Things are different when the “exception” is a specific provision in the policy or plan and
where there is something significant in the process that specifically makes the possible application
of the exception a main issue. An applicant or other process participant asking for an exception is
rarely going to make that a main issue in itself, so reasons don’t normally arise on foot of that alone.
But an inherently flexible policy with a specific “exception” requiring case-by-case treatment that is
relied on decisively by an institutional actor in the process to recommend a different result certainly
catapults the question of exceptions into the realm of being a main issue where reasons are required.
Such reasons are lacking here. I will return below to the somewhat more impactful status of
disagreeing with an institutional actor as opposed to merely not accepting a submission.
Lack of reasons in relation to design
111. As the applicant pleads at sub-grounds 13 and 14:
“13. At §3.2.10, the Inspector addresses the issue of exceptional design quality, noting
that the proposed architectural design had been addressed in detail in his previous reports
in the context of the 2023 Decision, which contained his finding that ‘the proposed design
incorporates sufficient architectural and visual interest to make a positive contribution to the
urban neighbourhood and streetscape.’
14. At §3.2.11, the Inspector noted that a Tall Building Statement, Visual Impact
Appraisal and Supplementary Appeal Document had been included in the submission made
on behalf of the Applicant. Relevantly, the Board’s Inspector noted as follows:
‘With particular reference to the Visual Impact Appraisal, I note and would concur with the
categorisation of viewpoint sensitivity at each of the chosen viewpoint locations as being low
to medium and would also concur, as has been set out in the previous reports, that the
development will be a significant intervention in a number of the available views. I also
agree with the submission that there is no sensitive aspect of the receiving environment that
requires protection from the development of a tall building.
[3.2.12.] To conclude, having examined the additional submissions received, I consider that
no significant new information or comment has been made that would result in a different
recommendation to that originally made in the Inspector’s Report dated 25th November
2022 and Addendum Report dated 5th December 2023 on Ref. ABP-313102-22.’”
112. The central complaint under this heading is pleaded as follows:
“22. While the Board Order states that it ‘did not share the Inspector’s view that
exceptional design quality had been demonstrated due to the mass, scale and form of the
proposed development and its relationship with the surrounding area’, no subtending reason
whatsoever is provided for this bald statement.”
113. The basic problem for the commission is that the inspector invested quite some effort and
detail in outlining the specifics of the development in terms of design, layout, finish, contribution to
place-making and other relevant aspects.

29
114. The vague, generic and formulaic reference to “mass, scale and form” as a reason for
rejecting the inspector’s view of design quality contrasts markedly with the latter’s detailed
examination of particular features of the development and falls more on the side of assertion rather
than legally sufficient reasoning when set against such a detailed analysis.
Lack of reasons in relation to car parking
115. The applicant resumes the attack on the reasoning of the decision under the car parking
heading. Again there is a striking contrast between the detailed criterion-by-criterion analysis of
the inspector and the generic dismissal by the commission.
116. In such circumstances I would uphold the complaint pleaded as follows:
“56. The Board’s basis for refusal in relation to car parking provision is grounded in the
finding that the Applicant ‘had not sufficiently demonstrated or justified the reduction in car
parking provision below the standards required in the development plan.’
57. However, the car parking provision standards set out in the GCDP, as described at
§11.1.1 and set out at Table 11.6 therein, are expressly stated to ‘be applied using
performance-based criteria appropriate to general location, in line with the National Planning
Framework (NPF) requirements. These standards will be subject to a range of tolerance that
enables alternative solutions to be proposed to achieve stated outcomes.’
58. The Board’s assessment did not take the provisions of the NPF, which were relevant
considerations, into account. At page 67 of the NPF, the following is outlined in relation to
standards:
‘In particular, general restrictions on building height or universal standards for car parking
or garden size may not be applicable in all circumstances in urban areas and should be
replaced by performance-based criteria appropriate to general location, e.g. city/town
centre, public transport hub, inner suburban, public transport corridor, outer suburban,
town, village etc.’
59. This policy approach is reflected in NPO 13, which was referenced in the Appeal
Submission at §3.1.1, and which states:
‘In urban areas, planning and related standards, including in particular building height and
car parking will be based on performance criteria that seek to achieve well-designed high
quality outcomes in order to achieve targeted growth. These standards will be subject to a
range of tolerance that enables alternative solutions to be proposed to achieve stated
outcomes, provided public safety is not compromised and the environment is suitably
protected.’
60. It is clear that it was precisely this approach which was followed by the Inspector in
addressing the issue of Car Parking Standards at §2.4.1. et seq. of the Addendum Report:
‘The development includes 193 No. car parking spaces, together with bicycle parking spaces.
A shuttle bus service was also proposed as part of the AI response submission. A
Transportation Assessment Report and Preliminary Mobility Management Plan was submitted
with the application.’
61. The Travel Plan concluded, at §§5.3 and 5.4, that:
‘the proposed development is appropriately located adjacent to transport facilities, including
bus services as well as employment zones and local facilities. It is also well served by
pedestrian and cycle linkages. The site is ideally placed to take advantage of non-car modes
of travel. Reduced car parking numbers are proposed for the scheme in line in compliance
with National Policy and guidance.
This report demonstrates that the proposed development will have an unnoticeable impact
upon the established local traffic conditions and can easily be accommodated on the road
network, with all commuter peak hour traffic increases being below TII recommended
threshold levels.’
62. At §§2.4.7. and 2.4.8., the Inspector noted that, the Travel Plan identified a
reasonable level of public transport requirements in line with National Planning Framework
criteria which require that car parking should be based on performance criteria that seek to
achieve well-designed high quality outcomes in order to achieve targeted growth. Having
reviewed the applicable bus timetables, the Inspector noted that ‘Each of these services
provides multiple stops across the city, including connections to other public transport
modes.’
63. At §2.4.9, the Inspector found that:
‘As I have outlined, the CDP parking standard is a maximum requirement and the CDP
Section 11.11.1 states that they will be subject to a range of tolerance that enables
alternative solutions to be proposed to achieve stated outcomes. Given the urban, accessible
location of the site, which is served by regular public transport, and where the applicant
proposes additional connectivity to public transport nodes in the City Centre, it is my view

30
that the proposed car parking facilities accord with the provisions of the National Planning
Framework and are acceptable.’
64. Insofar as the Board disagreed with this assessment, it failed to provide adequate
reasons, contrary to section 34(10)(b) of the 2000 Act. The Board did not provide any detail
beyond the mere assertion that the applicant had not sufficiently demonstrated or justified
the reduction in car parking provision below the standards required in the development
plan.”
117. Ultimately, the following problems arise in terms of lack of reasons under this heading:
(i) generally the commission’s stark disagreement is just assertion rather than a
reason;
(ii) there is no engagement with the detailed analysis by the inspector and no reason
given as to why the public transport services specifically identified by the inspector
do not constitute an adequate service;
(iii) there is no reference to the inspector’s reliance on city and national policy as a
reason for grant permission notwithstanding the shortfall in parking as against the
CDP, so no reasons were given for disagreement; and
(iv) nor does the commission make any reference to NPO 13 or address the performance
criteria as considered by the inspector.
Overall conclusion on reasons
118. Reasons have a particular resonance when the commission is disagreeing with planning
expertise at council level or in the inspectorate. The hierarchical relationship of the various roles
concerned means that the commission does indeed have the power to disagree, but they must do
so in an transparently reasoned way. Somewhat generic responses are insufficient even if they
might pass muster against a challenge by a disappointed process participant who received no support
for her position from institutional actors within the decision-making process. (I can add that such a
conclusion is consistent with the decision in Friends of the Irish Environment CLG & Anor. v. Minister
for Housing, Local Government and Heritage & Ors. [2025] IECA 128 (Unreported, Court of Appeal,
5 June 2024) per Hyland J. (Costello P. and Collins J. concurring) at para. 148, to the effect that it
is for the decision-taker in the first instance to decide whether submissions raise relevant issues;
but by contrast with that scenario, inherent objective features of the process such as the views of
institutional actors may of themselves demand reasoned engagement if the decision-taker is going
to disagree.) Here there is so much lacking from the decision, and so many of the inspector’s
perfectly reasonable points as to why permission should be granted have been airbrushed out of the
discussion altogether without response or even acknowledgement, that one can readily conclude
that there is pervasive and non-severable absence of reasons in the decision.
119. That said, and leaving aside any issue of law of interpretation of the CDP, I am not implying
that the commission’s evaluative perspectives were outside the range of the reasonable. Whether
this is a positive development, supporting economic growth, making the best use of the site and
providing a landmark on the approach to Galway city along the N6 (no more problematic than, say,
the RedClick Building on the N3 approach to Dublin city, within the Blanchardstown Retail Park –
which may or may not be a relevant comparison but which just comes to mind out of various possible
examples), or whether by contrast planning policy would be best served by a reduction in height
and rooms and a corresponding reduction in the exceedance of car-parking standards, is a matter
on which people may have to agree to differ. Quot homines, tot sententiae, as Terence allegedly
put it.
120. While I will be proposing remittal in the order, subject to the views of the parties, the
applicant is best placed to evaluate the pros and cons of pressing the first party appeal and/or of
proposing any other clarifications in the appeal context and/or of considering a new application to
the council as an alternative or parallel option. Conversely, while the commission isn’t required to
assist an applicant by conditioning a proposal, it would be unfortunate if an overly formal or precious
approach were to be taken to consideration of such options generally, perhaps as yet another
instance of the pendulum swinging to the contrary direction when difficulties arise. If a court can
on occasion attempt to identify options for practical resolution of cases and invite comment, it isn’t
unacceptable for other entities to do so either in relation to the decisions they have to make. At the
level of general principle there wouldn’t be anything problematic about a system which envisaged
the commission inviting participant’s views on, for example, conditioning height reductions, if that
is something they are minded to do and assuming that that can be accommodated within the
statutory framework, which I am not opining on. Whether that would make a project unviable is
something best coming from the applicant rather than the commission, and the fact that it makes a
potentially consented project materially different from that applied for is not in itself a reason for
refusal (because at the level of principle there isn’t anything impermissible about change in the
project in the course of the process) – it depends on whether the change gives rise to a real planning
issue that potential process participants could disagree with (like the last-minute change in White v.

31
Dublin City Council [2004] IESC 35, [2004] 1 I.R. 545, [2004] 2 I.L.R.M. 509 that introduced an
overlooking issue that had previously been considered by the third parties and found not to be an
issue: see the judgment of Fennelly J. (Murray, Mc Guinness and McCracken JJ. concurring)). If the
change is such that there is no basis to anticipate that it would be viewed as a worsening of the
position by any potential process participant (frequently height reduction comes into this category,
assuming the applicant for permission acquiesces), there may be a case for arguing that it just
absorbs energy and cost (for the commission and potentially for the court as well) to refuse and
then grant a subsequent modified application than to allow a conditioned project in the first place.
I appreciate the human tendency to overcompensate but there isn’t anything inherently suspect
about dialogue and consultation by public bodies, however independent, or at the level of principle
about modifying something as a result.
Other grounds
121. In such circumstances it is not necessary to deal with core grounds 2 or 5 or the balance of
core ground 4 (or indeed any points under core grounds 1 and 3 that are not dealt with above).
Summary
122. In outline summary, without taking from the more specific terms of this judgment:
(i) it is settled law that the commission must give adequate reasons for disagreeing
with its inspector; and
(ii) applying such law to the facts here, the commission did not do that.
Order
123. For the foregoing reasons it is ordered that:
(i) there be an order of certiorari by way of application for judicial review quashing the
order of the respondent, An Coimisiún Pleanála, made on 12 December 2024, to
refuse permission for the construction of, inter alia, an eight-storey over basement,
186-bedroom hotel with below ground pool plant equipment, tank room, standby
generator and pumping station (gross floor area: c. 12,478 m2) and associated
works on Site 4, Briarhill Business Park, Bóthar na dTreabh, the townlands of
Ballybrit and Doughiska, Galway (ABP-320582-24);
(ii) there be an order remitting both the first and third party appeals back to the
respondent to be considered and determined in accordance with this judgment, and
that in accordance with O. 103 r. 36(1)(a) RSC, subject to any contrary submission
of the parties within 7 days, the remittal be to the point in time immediately after
the inspector’s third report (dated 5 November 2024);
(iii) unless any party applies otherwise by the time and in the manner set out below, the
foregoing order be perfected forthwith thereafter on the basis of an order for costs
(including reserved costs if any and the costs of written submissions) being awarded
to the applicant against the respondent, and that any issue as to the extent of the
costs be determined, in default of agreement, in the legal costs adjudication process;
(iv) for the avoidance of doubt, the order for costs shall include an order for the costs of
legal costs adjudication;
(v) for the avoidance of doubt, any unsuccessful application to vary any provisional
order as to costs proposed in the judgment may be addressed pursuant to O. 103 r.
40(4) RSC;
(vi) the foregoing proposed orders as to costs, directions or the form of the order be
subject to contrary written legal submission within 7 days, with replies by other
parties within a further 7 days; and
(vii) the matter be listed on Monday 23 February 2026 to confirm the foregoing.
